speaker: interviewer
text: Yakob Basner.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is taken in Riga, 1934. On the left, Mina Gleser, my aunt, born name, [? Brockman. ?] On the right, my uncle, Chaim Gleser, who perished in Kaiserwald, concentration camp Kaiserwald Riga, 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In the previous picture, my Aunt Mina, with her two daughters, Yacha and Ida, perished in Rumbula, Riga. This picture is taken in 1936, 1937. And it's because of the visit of my grandmother, who is in the center. Sitting from left to right, my father, Leizer; my sister, Tsilya; brother, Abe; grandmother, came in for a visit from Daugavpils, Rivka--; cousin, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hirch Sher and other cousin, Fishke, and my uncle, Shleyme. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Standing from left to right-- my mother, Guta, me, and my Aunt Faga, all of them-- except I survived and the one on the right, Uncle Shleyme, who died in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture is taken in Riga at the end of 1945. I'm here not full 18 years old, returning from the concentration camps to Riga-- the first picture. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is taken in Riga, 1953. From the left to right, my wife, Doba; youngest daughter, Elizabeth; older daughter, Guta, who is named after my mother, and Elizabeth after my father, and me. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] This picture is taken in Riga in 1974. It's my Aunt's Mina's sister-- her name Faga Jacobson, born [? Brockman. ?] She emigrated to Russia and survived there. She helped me a lot after I returned from the camps. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture is taken in 1995 in one of the synagogues of San Diego, where the bar mitzvah of my youngest grandson, Danny, took place. That standing from left to right-- that's me, Yakob Basner; my wife, Doba Basner; my older daughter, Guta Basner, and my grandson, the bar mitzvah boy, Denny. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This picture is taken in 1995 in California birthday of my younger daughter. From left to right-- my older daughter Guta, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my son-in-law, [? Ilya, ?] Elizabeth's husband; Elizabeth, her daughter, Elizabeth's daughter, Lea the last name Karpukh, all three of them-- [? Ilya, ?] Elizabeth, and Lea Karpukh. And the second husband of my older daughter Guta, David [? Sharpov. ?] Then from left to right standing-- my younger grandson, Danny. Sitting from left to right-- that's me, Yakob Basner and my wife, Doba Basner. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1993 in Long Beach, California. It's my older grandson with his wife. Grandson-- [INAUDIBLE] Fleyshman and his wife, Helen Fleyshman. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us why you came to the United States rather than going to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my dream to go to Israel began actually when I was, as I told already the story, when I was five years old, and I was sent to Israel by the nurse in there-- in children's hospital. And so my-- my dream and goal all the time to go to Israel. But it changed in 1979 when our daughter, the youngest daughter went to the United States. So we want to be together with our children, and we came to the United States. And then our older daughter came in 1982. So all my whole family is together. And as we came to the United States, I began to fulfill my dreams about perpetuating the Yiddish language. I told already about the language, what meaning it had for me and for the people who perished. I felt that as-- as a duty, as-- I have to do it. And from the beginning, I did everything to accomplish it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to work. I was working in the same trade as my father-- a little different here in-- in Long Beach. I couldn't find the same job. But after three weeks I was here, I went to work and worked until recently as a laser cutter by garments in Long Beach. And I began to-- to-- to participate in the social life of the Jewish Community Center in Long Beach and also performing in different places, reading-- readings, lectures, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a few years, I began to teach Yiddish-- in the beginning, in Long Beach Community Center, and after that, in the Workman's Circle or Arbeter Ring, in Los Angeles. And in 1993, I received my credentials, teacher's credentials, and I began to teach in Beverly Hills Adult High School, where I have more than 60 students, three classes. I also teach-- teached and teach different organizations and institutions and also lecturing in the Yiddish Cultural Club of Los Angeles, Yiddish Cultural Club of Orange County, and in other places. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So now, actually, I feel the only place where I could feel free I could do what I like to do and feeling then I do the right thing. Even it was hard because when we came-- in my age, when I came, I was already 53 years old, and it is hard to start a new life. The culture is different. The language was unknown. I didn't know the language. So we had to start everything from the beginning. But I had the energy. My family helped me-- my wife. And we achieved what we-- what I-- I was dreaming about. And I'm glad I'm here. I'm also going to Israel. I went to Israel in 1981 after less than a year I was here to the first gathering of the survivors in Jerusalem and Tel Aviv. I met a lot of friends from all over the world-- from South Africa, from England when we were together in the camps. And I went to other places. And everywhere I lectured. I met people, talked about the Holocaust, and in 1990, after 10 years, I went to Riga. And the Yiddish-- the Yiddish Club, I was witness-- witnessing the return of the Yiddish Club, where I-- where I was, as a child, in the Yiddish Theater. And later in the-- during the Nazi occupation, I was there risking my life to get some food there. And now, I was witnessing the moment when this cultural building-- the building was what builded by Jewish-- by the Jewish community was returned to the Jewish people. And I had the honor to perform. One of the-- the first to have a representation there and to read for them what American Jewish writers and also my writing, what I wrote for the-- for in Yiddish and answer many, many questions. I attended all the places-- took pictures and videos-- from the ghetto, on Kaiserwald, in Rumbula, in Salispils, in Bikerneku, everywhere where Jewish people perished. And there is a lot of places. The soil in Latvia is full with Jewish people buried there. It was hard, but I felt I have to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is the life in the United States. My children are working. They are coming together. Our family is very close. And I try-- I try to continue the traditions what we had, and to introduce them to the tradition, and to do-- to love them and to feel what-- how important they are to our life. Because if not the traditions, our Jewish traditions, we wouldn't survive as a people for many-- for thousands of years.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I hope that the children will follow in our steps. I'm also conducting a Russian-- a Russian family circle at the Long Beach Community Center to introduce people who never attended a Yiddish Seder, or a Hanukkah celebration, or a Purim celebration, Passover, and so on and so on. And you feel good when you can introduce them in people 80 years old. And they attended a Seder when they were eight years old. And they came and thank you. And you feel then you did something good for myself and for the people. And we have a Hamish event in the Yiddish Community Center, in the Jewish Community Center in Long Beach, for the beginning when I-- when I came from-- a year maybe after I came until now. It's the longest program who is surviving-- we use a lot of survivors today-- surviving-- who is surviving in Long Beach in the community center. And it is completely in Yiddish. Even I teach them some English words every time. And the people who attend and come, they know-- they know a lot more about Yiddish culture, about Yiddish literature, about Yiddish traditions, and I feel good about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to mention that award or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in 1986, I won an award from HIAS from the Judge Murray Gurfain Award for Adoption to US Life. And I received this award from the Jewish Community Center for Outstanding Voluntary Service. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Want the important-- the importance about the interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: One more thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: What it means to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, all right. All right. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few words about the importance of the interview and what I expected and how  I weighted it. You know, the one-- one of the reasons why we survived-- sure, there was luck or-- or incidentals occasion go on. When you are sent to this, you can be the smartest, the strongest, the best one-- you'll get killed. But in between to survive  from one selection to the other, you need hope. You need-- I think that also my upbringing helped me. I never lost my dignity and self-respect. I hoped all the time that somehow some-- some miracle will happen, and we'll survive. I'll survive. And then, when I survive, I'll be able to tell the people what happened. I felt it as a duty to they who-- to them who perished. And wherever I could-- it's hard to talk about it-- but I did it to-- talking to-- to children, to students at schools, different institutions, and organizations. I wrote my memories in the book from the Latvian survivors-- survivors of-- Jewish survivors of Latvia--

speaker: interviewer
text: I wrote some articles in the newspapers. And-- The-- the interview-- one of eight, too-- if it will help some people to understand the Holocaust-- most of it important in the time when some people deny it even when we are alive. What will be after our deaths, when we'll be gone? So it has to help the people to be-- not to be indifferent what happened in the world, most of all, to Jewish people, and to pursue and to-- to be active, to fight against anti-Semitism, against the smallest part of it if, you know, if you see somewhere, because we saw what was happened in-- in the Holocaust. And it could-- it can happen again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody's assured that it will not happen. It will not help your-- changing your last name or your first name or whatever you'll do or assimilate. You are a Jew, and you will be. So if it helps a little, I am glad to do it. It's hard to do, but I'm glad I did it. I was waiting for this long, long years.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
